learning_objectives: Define the intelligence cycle and its application to cyber threats
Distinguish between strategic, operational, and tactical intelligence
Identify threat intelligence sources and collection methods

lecture_notes.introduction: Threat intelligence transforms raw data about adversaries into actionable knowledge that improves security decision-making across all organizational levels.
key_concepts: Intelligence cycle: Direction, Collection, Processing, Analysis, Dissemination, Feedback
Intelligence levels: strategic (board), operational (SOC management), tactical (analysts)
Collection sources: OSINT, commercial feeds, ISAC sharing, dark web monitoring
Diamond Model: Adversary, Infrastructure, Capability, Victim
Kill chain and ATT&CK integration
lecture_notes.demonstration: Collecting threat intelligence on a specific threat actor using OSINT tools and structuring it using the Diamond Model

review.value: Relates four core features of an intrusion: Adversary, Infrastructure, Capability, and Victim. Enables pivot analysis: knowing one feature helps discover others.
Strategic: 'APT29 is targeting healthcare organizations for COVID-19 research.' Tactical: 'Block these 15 IP addresses and 8 file hashes associated with APT29 infrastructure.'

learning_objectives: Profile threat actors by capability, intent, and targeting
Apply analytical frameworks for attribution assessment
Evaluate confidence levels in attribution claims
readings: APT reports from Mandiant, CrowdStrike, and Kaspersky

lecture_notes.introduction: Understanding who is behind an attack enables prediction of future behavior and targeted defensive measures. Attribution is challenging but valuable when properly qualified.
key_concepts: Threat actor taxonomy: nation-state, criminal, hacktivist, insider
Capability assessment: tooling sophistication, operational security, resources
Intent analysis: targeting patterns, timing correlation with events
Attribution evidence: malware code overlap, infrastructure reuse, TTPs
Confidence levels: low, moderate, high with supporting evidence requirements
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing an APT report to extract threat actor profile elements, mapping TTPs to MITRE ATT&CK, and assessing attribution confidence

review.value: Sophisticated actors plant evidence to misdirect attribution: embedding language strings from other nations, reusing known malware from different groups, mimicking TTPs of rival actors.
High confidence requires multiple independent evidence types (code overlap + infrastructure reuse + targeting pattern + HUMINT). Single evidence types support only low confidence.

class_schedule.topic: Collection and Processing: OSINT and Dark Web
learning_objectives: Conduct OSINT collection for threat intelligence purposes
Monitor dark web forums and marketplaces safely
Process and normalize collected intelligence data

lecture_notes.introduction: Collection is the foundation of the intelligence cycle. The quality of analysis depends entirely on the breadth and reliability of collected data.
key_concepts: OSINT collection: social media, paste sites, code repositories, CT logs
Dark web monitoring: Tor-based forums, marketplaces, paste sites
Operational security for collection: anonymization, identity separation
Data processing: deduplication, normalization, enrichment
Source reliability assessment: Admiralty Code (A1-F6)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Conducting a structured OSINT collection operation against a simulated threat actor, correlating across multiple sources
setup_requirements: Tor Browser for dark web access (controlled lab)

review.value: Two-dimensional rating: Source reliability (A-Completely reliable to F-Cannot be judged) and Information credibility (1-Confirmed to 6-Cannot be judged). An A1 rating is the highest confidence.
Use dedicated VMs, VPN chains, and separate identities for dark web monitoring. Never access from corporate networks. Maintain operational security to avoid alerting threat actors to monitoring.

learning_objectives: Apply structured analytic techniques to threat intelligence
Use Analysis of Competing Hypotheses (ACH) for attribution
Recognize and mitigate cognitive biases in analysis
readings: Structured Analytic Techniques for Intelligence Analysis

lecture_notes.introduction: Raw data is not intelligence. Analysis transforms data into insight through rigorous methodology that accounts for uncertainty, bias, and competing explanations.
key_concepts: Analysis of Competing Hypotheses (ACH): systematic evaluation of alternative explanations
Cognitive biases: confirmation bias, anchoring, availability heuristic
Structured brainstorming and red teaming
Confidence assessment and communication of uncertainty
Intelligence writing: BLUF (Bottom Line Up Front) format
lecture_notes.demonstration: Applying ACH to a threat attribution problem with conflicting evidence, evaluating three competing hypotheses

review.value: List hypotheses, identify evidence, evaluate each evidence item against each hypothesis (consistent/inconsistent/not applicable), eliminate hypotheses with the most inconsistent evidence. Focus on disconfirmation, not confirmation.
Tendency to favor information that confirms existing beliefs. Mitigate by: assigning devil's advocate, requiring evidence that would disconfirm the hypothesis, ACH methodology.

learning_objectives: Create and consume STIX 2.1 threat intelligence objects
Configure TAXII servers for automated intelligence sharing
Participate in ISACs and trust groups

lecture_notes.introduction: Threat intelligence increases in value when shared. Standardized formats and automated sharing protocols enable organizations to benefit from collective defense.
key_concepts: STIX 2.1 objects: Indicator, Malware, Threat Actor, Attack Pattern, Campaign
STIX relationships and sighting reports
TAXII 2.1: collections, channels, and API endpoints
ISACs: sector-specific sharing communities
Traffic Light Protocol (TLP) for sharing classification
lecture_notes.demonstration: Creating STIX 2.1 intelligence objects from a malware analysis report and publishing them to a TAXII server for automated consumption

review.value: Objects are connected via relationship objects: Malware 'uses' Attack-Pattern, Threat-Actor 'attributed-to' Intrusion-Set, Indicator 'indicates' Malware. Relationships create a threat knowledge graph.

Traffic Light Protocol: TLP:RED (named recipients only), TLP:AMBER (organization + need-to-know), TLP:AMBER+STRICT (organization only), TLP:GREEN (community), TLP:CLEAR (public). Governs redistribution.

learning_objectives: Integrate threat intelligence into security operations workflows

lecture_notes.introduction: Threat intelligence only has value when it drives action. Operationalization ensures that intelligence products are consumed by the right people to make better security decisions.
key_concepts: Priority Intelligence Requirements (PIRs): what leadership needs to know
Intelligence integration: SIEM rules, firewall blocks, hunting queries
Feedback loop: operational results informing collection priorities
Program metrics: IoC hit rate, MTTD improvement, hunting success rate
Analyst workflow: daily intelligence briefing, weekly threat report
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building an automated intelligence pipeline: TAXII feed to SIEM correlation rule to automatic firewall block, with analyst review dashboard

review.value: Priority Intelligence Requirements are questions leadership needs answered: 'Which threat actors target our industry?' 'What new TTPs are being used against our technology stack?' PIRs drive the entire intelligence cycle.

Percentage of consumed IoCs that trigger alerts in the environment. Very low hit rate (<0.1%) may indicate intelligence is not relevant. Very high rate may indicate known threats already detected by other means.

quizzes.topic: Intelligence Cycle, Actor Profiling, and Collection

questions.question: You receive a report that a threat actor is selling access to your organization's VPN on a dark web forum. The post includes screenshots of internal network shares. Apply the Diamond Model to this scenario, identify what you know about each vertex, and describe your intelligence collection plan to fill gaps.
questions.answer: Diamond Model analysis: Adversary: Unknown; selling access suggests financial motivation (cybercriminal). Infrastructure: The dark web forum (collection target for actor profiling), and the compromised VPN endpoint. Capability: VPN access, internal network navigation (indicates credential compromise or VPN vulnerability exploitation). Victim: Our organization. Collection plan: (1) Forum monitoring: track the actor's post history, other sales, communication style for profiling. (2) VPN logs: identify which VPN account was compromised, source IPs, authentication timestamps. (3) Internal network logs: determine the scope of access from the screenshots (which shares were accessed). (4) Credential checks: verify if the compromised credentials appear in recent breach databases. Gaps to fill: actor identity, initial access method, how long they have had access, whether access was already sold to others.

rubric.key: Specific collection plan for each gap

questions.question: Explain the difference between an Indicator of Compromise (IoC) and a TTP. Why are TTPs more valuable for defense than IoCs, and give a specific example where IoC-based detection fails but TTP-based detection succeeds.
questions.answer: IoCs are specific observable artifacts: IP addresses, domain names, file hashes. They are easy to change (the adversary registers a new domain, recompiles the malware). TTPs are the behaviors and methods: how the attacker gains initial access, escalates privileges, moves laterally. TTPs are harder to change because they reflect the attacker's skill set and tooling. Example: A malware hash IoC detects version 1.0 of an implant. The attacker recompiles with a minor change, producing a completely new hash. IoC-based detection fails. However, the malware still uses process hollowing (T1055.012) to inject into svchost.exe and communicates via DNS TXT records (T1071.004). TTP-based detection (Sysmon rule detecting process hollowing + DNS analytics detecting anomalous TXT query volume) succeeds regardless of the binary's hash.

rubric.key: Clear distinction between IoCs and TTPs
Explains why TTPs are more durable
Specific example where IoC fails and TTP succeeds

questions.question: You have evidence suggesting a breach was conducted by either APT28 (Russian) or APT35 (Iranian). Evidence: (1) Malware contains Russian-language strings, (2) C2 infrastructure hosted in Iran, (3) Targeting aligns with Iranian geopolitical interests, (4) Code reuses a tool publicly attributed to APT28. Apply Analysis of Competing Hypotheses to evaluate these two hypotheses.
questions.answer: ACH Matrix: Evidence 1 (Russian strings): Consistent with APT28, Inconsistent with APT35 (but could be a false flag). Evidence 2 (Iranian infrastructure): Inconsistent with APT28 (but could use third-party hosting), Consistent with APT35. Evidence 3 (Iranian geopolitical targeting): Inconsistent with APT28, Consistent with APT35. Evidence 4 (APT28 tool reuse): Consistent with APT28, Neutral for APT35 (the tool was publicly leaked, anyone could use it). Analysis: APT28 has one strongly consistent evidence (Russian strings) but two inconsistent (Iranian infra, Iranian targeting). APT35 has two strongly consistent (infrastructure, targeting) with one inconsistent (Russian strings, likely false flag). Evidence 4 is weak because the tool is publicly available. Conclusion: APT35 is the stronger hypothesis with moderate confidence. The Russian strings are likely a false flag operation.

rubric.key: Proper handling of potentially deceptive evidence
Justified conclusion with appropriate confidence level

questions.question: Design three Priority Intelligence Requirements (PIRs) for the CISO of a mid-size financial services firm. For each PIR, specify: the question, who consumes the answer, what action it drives, and how collection would be performed.
questions.answer: PIR 1: 'Which threat actors are actively targeting the financial services sector and what initial access vectors are they using?' Consumer: SOC management. Action: Adjust detection rules and hunting priorities to focus on current attack vectors. Collection: FS-ISAC advisories, commercial TI feeds filtered for financial sector, dark web monitoring for financial institution access sales. PIR 2: 'Are any of our organization's credentials, systems, or data currently for sale or exposed on criminal marketplaces?' Consumer: CISO and IR team. Action: Immediate credential reset and incident investigation if positive. Collection: Dark web marketplace monitoring, credential breach database checks, domain monitoring on paste sites. PIR 3: 'What emerging vulnerabilities affect our critical technology stack (Oracle DB, F5 BIG-IP, Citrix ADC) and are they being actively exploited?' Consumer: Vulnerability management team. Action: Emergency patching for actively exploited vulnerabilities. Collection: CVE monitoring with technology filter, CISA KEV catalog, exploit broker marketplace monitoring.

rubric.key: Consumer and action clearly defined for each

major_assignments.title: Threat Intelligence Report and Program Design
major_assignments.description: Produce a comprehensive threat intelligence report on a specific threat actor and design a threat intelligence program for the organization to defend against them.
objectives: Research and profile a threat actor using OSINT and published reports
Map the actor's TTPs to MITRE ATT&CK
Develop detection and hunting strategies based on the actor's TTPs
Design a threat intelligence program with PIRs and sharing strategy
deliverables: Threat actor profile and intelligence report
MITRE ATT&CK mapping with detection recommendations
Threat intelligence program proposal with PIRs
STIX 2.1 package of all identified IoCs and TTPs

criteria: Comprehensive profile with Diamond Model analysis
TTPs mapped to ATT&CK with technique IDs
Hunting hypotheses based on actor behavior
Professional threat intelligence report with rigorous analysis
Actionable detection strategy based on actor TTPs

weekly_quizzes.description: Assessment of threat intelligence concepts and analysis techniques

lab_assignments.description: OSINT collection, analysis exercises, and STIX/TAXII labs

major_assignments.description: Threat intelligence report and program design

midterm_examination.description: Assessment covering intelligence cycle, actor profiling, and analysis

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam covering all threat intelligence topics

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all class sessions. More than 2 unexcused absences may result in a grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late without prior arrangement.
course_policies.collaboration: Intelligence discussions encouraged. All reports and analysis must be individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Research must use publicly available sources. Do not access restricted threat intelligence platforms without authorization.
course_policies.lab_safety: Dark web access is only permitted within the controlled lab environment. Follow all OPSEC procedures for collection activities.
